---
title: "Foxtail Bog Clubmoss (Lycopodiella alopecuroides)"
slug: "lycopodiella-alopecuroides"
group: "Plantae"
image: "https://inaturalist-open-data.s3.amazonaws.com/photos/180847530/medium.jpeg"        # keep for thumbnails
image-alt: "Foxtail Bog Clubmoss"  # keep for a11y
tax_kingdom: "Plantae"
tax_class: "Lycopodiopsida"
tax_order: "Lycopodiales"
tax_family: "Lycopodiaceae"
categories: ["Plantae", "Lycopodiopsida", "Lycopodiales", "Lycopodiaceae"]   # these drive the chips + filtering
freeze: true
---

<figure class="species-figure">

<img src="https://inaturalist-open-data.s3.amazonaws.com/photos/180847530/medium.jpeg" alt="Foxtail Bog Clubmoss" class="species-photo"/>

<figcaption class="photo-credit">

Photo: <a href="https://www.inaturalist.org/people/deanna_d">
deanna_d</a> (CC BY-NC) via <a href="https://www.inaturalist.org/observations/107518319">iNaturalist</a>

</figcaption>

</figure>

## Taxonomy

-   **Class:** Lycopodiopsida
-   **Order:** Lycopodiales
-   **Family:** Lycopodiaceae

## Occurrence at DeLuca Preserve

-   [DeLuca Bio Blitz Species Observations](https://www.inaturalist.org/observations?project_id=191727&subview=map&taxon_id=122049)

## Conservation & rarity context

-   IUCN: Not Listed
-   **Rarity note:** Common species in Florida found in moist to wet, sandy habitats such as bogs, wet flatwoods, and seepage areas.

## Natural history notes

A distinctive, low-growing plant characterized by its trailing stems and upright, cone-like spore structures that resemble a fox’s tail. In Florida, it is found in moist to wet, sandy habitats such as bogs, wet flatwoods, and seepage areas. It grows during the warmer months and reproduces by spores, often forming scattered patches in open wetland environments.

## References / links

-   [iNaturalist species page](https://www.inaturalist.org/taxa/122049-Lycopodiella-alopecuroides)
-   [Local profile](https://florida.plantatlas.usf.edu/plant/species/3761)
